details.text: in addition to being from al-huroof al-muqatta’aat (separated/disjointed letters) which has been elaborated upon in previous surahs, there is an opinion that يس is in reference to the prophet muhammad ﷺ in the case where the ياء refers to the نِداء (call) i.e., يَا (like “o” prophet), the سِين is indicative of the human being and humanity due to it being the central letter of the word إِنْسان (human) and the prophet ﷺ is considered to represent the centre and essence of humanity.
Derivative: اتَّبَعَ means to follow and implies a following of something that appeals to the person and that he has an openness towards.

- “And he fears Ar-Rahman (The Most Merciful) بِالْغَيْبِ”. Here بِالْغَيْبِ can mean “Allah in the unseen” i.e., he fears Allah although he cannot see Him or although he can’t see His punishment, or it can mean “(when) I am unseen” i.e., when nobody can see me.
Derivative: آثَارَ – Refers to traces, left over remnants and footsteps in sand. Here, it refers to what you left behind and the consequences of your actions even after you are gone.
Derivative: أَحْصَيْنَا from أَحْصَى which means to count something, encompass it and have a full record of it.
Derivative: إِمَامٍ can hold multiple meanings. One meaning is a wide road and here it can refer to the book where records are kept which is a like a wide road, like wide scrolls that keep unrolling.

Derivative: الْمُرْسَلُونَ literally means those who were sent. It could therefore mean either those whom Allah sent, or those whom a Messenger sent to undertake a task or engage in Da’wah. The most widely held view is that it refers to Messengers whom Allah sent and the fact that the plural was used indicates that there were more than two.

Derivative: الْبَلَاغُ is from بَلَغَ which means to reach. From that the meaning of a communication (بَلَاغ) was derived, referring to a means to make information reach others. Here, it is referring to the message that they have been sent to deliver. بَلَاغ also implies the meaning of words that are so powerful and reaching that they penetrate the chest and hit a person’s heart i.e., a powerful communique or the imparting of a strong or impactful message.

Derivative: تَطَيَّرْنَا – from طَيْر which means a bird. تَطَيَّرَmeans to attribute a curse based on the origin of a bird, in the case where a crow was seen as a sign of being cursed. Therefore, the use of تَطَيَّرْنَا here means that “we see a bad omen in you” or “We see an evil omen or curse from you”.
Derivative: طَائِرُ – “Bird” referring to curse and evil open. Here, in the context of طَائِرُكُم مَّعَكُمْ it means “Your curse or evil omen is with you”.
Derivative: مُّسْرِفُونَ means to be over extravagant and in excess. Here it implies that you are a people or nation that “goes too far” or “exceeds the limits”.

Derivative: وَ is وَاوُ القَسْم “The Waw of the oath or swearing of the oath”. Additional points about the oath: In old Arabic, the oath served two additional functions different from those common today. The first was to draw the attention of the audience to make it known that what he is about to say is important or urgent. In this case he would swear by an object like a mountain, tribe, moon, you etc. What he swears by what provide an indication of the level of importance of what he is about to say. The Qur’an added another dimension to oaths unique to the Qur’an and is used in a manner that cannot be found elsewhere. Allah made the object of the oath to serve as a witness or evidence to corroborate the subject of the oath. An example of this is:
وَٱلۡعَصۡرِ (1) إِنَّ ٱلۡإِنسَٰنَ لَفِي خُسۡرٍ
By time * Verily, the human being is in loss
It’s like saying: “Time is a witness or testifies to the fact that human beings are in loss” or “That time itself is proof and evidence that human beings are in loss i.e., referring to the fact that time is the biggest loss that humans suffer and cannot escape”. Here, وَالْقُرْآنِ الْحَكِيمِ relates to the object of the oath: “By the Quran that is full of wisdom” and the next verse “إِنَّكَ لَمِنَ الْمُرْسَلِينَ” – “Verily, you are from the Messengers” is the subject of the oath. As such, the Qur’an is enough of a witness and proof that you (O Muhammad) are from the Messengers. The Qur’an is a witness on the Day of Judgement that He is a Messenger and it is a proof that he is a Messenger because the Qur’an that he brought could only have come from Allah.

- Literally means: “And what is to me” and here it carries the meaning: “And what should prevent me from (worshipping the One who create me)” or “And why should I not (worship the One who created me)”.
Derivative: يُنقِذُونِ from أَنْقَذَ which means to rescue or save.
Derivative: مَا in بِمَا here is مَا المَصْدَرِيَة which means that it is like أَن, so that it means: “That”.

Derivative: خَامِدُونَ means to be lifeless and still. From the verb خَمَدَ which means to go out and die down (in respect to a fire) or to subside and abate. Here it is an expression for them becoming lifeless and still.
“verily, you are the messengers” – this is the subject of the oath in the previous verse.

Derivative: نَسْلَخُ from سَلَخَ which means to strip off. It is used to mean to remove the skin or hide of an animal, for something to depart from something and it can mean that a period of time reaches its end. Here it is referring to the day (and its light) being stripped and made depart from the night, so that the people are left in darkness.
Derivative: قَدَّرْنَاهُ from قَدَّرَ which means to decree or calculate and provide with its precise measures or rules.
Derivative: مَنَازِلَ refers to stages or phases i.e., the place that it proceeds to each night.
Derivative: الْعُرْجُونِ refers the bent bark of a palm tree or a date-palm stalk that is curved. Here it is being compared to the curved arched shape of the moon.

Derivative: ذُرِّيَّة commonly refers to offspring and progeny but it can conversely also mean grandfathers and ancestors, which is its meaning here according to the majority of the scholars of Tafsir.
Derivative: صَرِيخَ from صَرَخَ which means to cry out or scream. صَرِيخَ refers to a cry out in distress to be saved.

Derivative: تَنزِيلَ “A revelation” and refers to revelation that keeps coming down in a gradual manner. تَنزِيلَ here is in the مَنْصوب (Mansoob-Accusative) case with a Fat’hah. It is the مَفْعول بِهِ in the case where the فِعْل (verb) has been omitted, where the omitted part could be “Allah revealed” (نَزَّلَ اللهُ تَنزِيلَ). In other Qiraa’aat (preserved recitations) it has come as تَنزِيلُ with a Dammah in the مَرْفوع (Marfoo’ – Nominative) state in which case it would be referring to the Qur’an i.e., “The Qur’an is تَنزِيلُ الْعَزِيزِ الرَّحِيمِ.
Derivative: تَوْصِيَةً from وَصَّى which means to make a will or give last instructions or recommendations before death.
Derivative: يَنسِلُونَ from نَسَلَ which in this context means to walk in a rush like someone who is rushing due to fear.

Derivative: الْأَجْدَاثِ is the plural of جَدَث and means a grave which no longer has its markings, which implies that people from across the ages will be raised from their graves when the trumpet is blown which the people of later generations did not even know were graves.
Derivative: وَيْل carries the meaning of “there cannot be something greater or worse than that” or “thing’s couldn’t possibly be worse”.
Derivative: مَّرْقَدِ is from رَقَدَ which means to sleep (lightly) or to lie down to rest. مَّرْقَدِ is the place of sleep or rest.

Derivative: فَاكِهُونَ means happy, joyous and cheerful. It refers to someone’s state when something puts a smile onto their face. From it we get the word فَاكِهَة (fruit) because when you bite into it, you get a smile on your face and brings you joy.
Derivative: شُغُلٍ means to be occupied, busy doing things and engaged in activity.
Derivative: امْتَازُوا from اِمْتازَ which means to be distinct and distinguished. Here, its meaning is “O criminals, separate yourselves and be cut off today (i.e., from the believers)”.
Derivative: غَافِلُونَ from غَفَلَ – غَفْلَة which means to be in a carefree and heedless state.
Derivative: جِبِلًّا can mean an Ummah or a group of the people and a creation or multitudes from the creation i.e., a large portion of the creation (mankind).

Derivative: طَمَسْنَا from طَمَسَ which means to obliterate, eradicate, erase, or to wipe, blot or rub something out until no sign of it is left remaining. Here, it refers to the complete removal of sight or ability to see through pulverising the eyes until there is nothing of them left.

Derivative: مَسَخْنَا from مَسَخَ which means to disfigure or to transform the creation from one state to another (like into animals or a rock). The meaning here is that: “If We so wish, We can transform them into another ugly form that makes them unable to move from their place.

Derivative: نُنَكِّسْ from نَكَّسَ which means to make something regress, deteriorate, reverse or roll back. Here it refers to the degeneration when Allah causes someone to reach an elderly age and becomes weak and decrepit. At that age he regresses and returns to the state that he began in when he was a baby and young child.

Derivative: الشِّعْرَ means poetry however in the context it carries a wider meaning in the case where poetry (شِعْر) was synonymous with entertainment. Therefore, the accusation that the Messenger ﷺ brought poetry or that he was a poet were accusations that took away from the seriousness of the message he brought.

- Literal meaning: “The word has come into effect”. The meaning of (الْقَوْلُ) here is “the verdict” and the meaning of حَقَّ is to become deserved, mandated and obligatory (upon them) – It has been passed.

Derivative: ذَلَّلْنَا from ذَلَّلَ which means to subdue and humble. Here, it means that Allah made the livestock and riding animals upon a nature that man could make use of them in terms of riding and eating. He created them easy to make use of.

Derivative: خَصِيمٌ is in the فَعِيل form and refers to someone who is an antagonist and opponent who argues intensely and continuously in an arrogant manner. Someone who is always looking to argue and contest.
Derivative: أَغْلَالًا refers to shackles and fetters, like the chained collars that were placed upon the necks of prisoners.
Derivative: الْأَذْقَانِ which is the plural of ذَقَن or ذِقَن which means chin.

Derivative: مُّقْمَحُونَ from أَقْمَحَ which means for heads to be raised or held up whilst the eyes are directed down (in shame). Here, it demonstrates that even though the truth is right before them, they are unable to see it i.e., They can’t see what’s right in front of them because they can’t lower their heads due to the chains that have been placed around their necks reaching to their chins.
Derivative: الْخَلَّاقُ – The Creator who creates over and over again.

- شَيْئًا comes from شَاءَ to will something. When you have a strong will, it materialises something and that thing is شَيْء. إِرَادَة from أَرادَ and is a will and intention that is less than شَاءَ in the Arabic language, however in respect to Allah His إِرَادَة (will/intention) is شَيْئًا i.e., it materialises. There is no such thing as a weak intention (إِرَادَة) in respect to Allah. If he أَرَادَ شَيْئًا (wills or intends a thing), He says to it “Be” and it comes into existence!
